title: Drive sequence reshaping with an explicit result type for `split`
intent: When one input sequence can be partitioned in multiple ways, fix the intended structure by annotating the result type.
pattern_summary: Use `split` together with an explicit result type or local signature so Cryptol knows both the number of parts and the size of each part.
pattern_template:
<name> xs = ys
  where
    ys : [parts][each]<elem_type>
    ys = split xs
rationale: `split` is highly polymorphic. Without result context, Cryptol may report a polymorphic value or leave the intended partition ambiguous.
positive_example:
msg' : [diameter][row][8]
msg' = split msg
variation_notes: May also be written as `split xs : [parts][each]a` at the use site.
Useful before `transpose`, `join`, or round/state conversions.
anti_pattern: split xs   // no result context when multiple valid partitions exist
constraints: The input must have type `[parts * each]a`.
The inner length typically requires `fin each`.
applicable_when: reshaping a flat byte/message sequence into rows or blocks
converting between flat state and matrix/state forms
repairing `Cannot evaluate polymorphic value` after using `split`

title: Implement matrix-style transposition as `join (transpose msg')`
intent: Express row/column reordering by splitting into a matrix, transposing, and joining back.
pattern_summary: First split a flat sequence into a 2D structure, then apply `transpose`, and finally flatten with `join`. This is the standard Cryptol pattern for matrix-like reordering.
pattern_template:
transform msg = join (transpose msg')
  where
    msg' : [outer][inner]a
    msg' = split msg
rationale: This pattern appears naturally whenever an algorithm conceptually writes data by rows and reads it by columns, or vice versa.
positive_example:
scytale msg = join (transpose msg')
  where
    msg' : [diameter][row][8]
    msg' = split msg
variation_notes: Decryption/inverse direction is often the same expression but with a different local signature on `msg'`.
Can be reused for state/message conversions in block ciphers.
anti_pattern: Writing nested indexing logic manually when the operation is conceptually just a reshape + transpose + flatten.
constraints: The intermediate matrix dimensions must multiply to the original length.
`transpose : [n][m]a -> [m][n]a` preserves element order up to matrix transpose.
applicable_when: converting between message order and state order

title: Treat words as sequences of bits and reuse sequence operators
intent: Reuse generic sequence operators on words because `[n]` is just `[n]Bit`.
pattern_summary: In Cryptol, finite words are sequences of bits, so sequence operations such as `take`, `drop`, `split`, `#`, and indexing apply directly to words.
pattern_template:
hi = take`{k} w
lo = drop`{k} w
parts = split w
rationale: This pattern is fundamental for parsing bytes, fields, and bit slices from packed words without inventing a separate representation.
variation_notes: Often combine with concatenation `#` for packing/unpacking fields.
Use explicit widths when the default width of decimal literals would be ambiguous.
anti_pattern: Converting a word into an ad hoc tuple/record first when simple sequence primitives suffice.
constraints: Remember Cryptol words are big-endian in the sense used by its shift/sequence interpretation.
Operations stay subject to width-sensitive typing.
applicable_when: rewriting pseudocode that speaks about high bits and low bits

title: Use comprehensions for structured sequence construction instead of manual indexing
intent: Build derived sequences declaratively from one or more source sequences.
pattern_summary: Use list-style comprehensions when each output element is an expression over one or more input streams or arrays. Parallel branches act like zipped traversal.
pattern_template: [ <expr> | x <- xs | y <- ys ]
rationale: Comprehensions match the style of many cryptographic specs and avoid manual index plumbing.
positive_example: [ (x, y) | x <- [1 .. 3 : Integer] | y <- [4 : Integer, 5 : Integer] ]
variation_notes: Use cartesian branches for nested products.
Use parallel branches for lockstep traversal.
`generate` is the lower-level primitive when you want explicit indices.
anti_pattern: Hand-written repeated indexing like `xs @ i`, `ys @ i` throughout when the computation is pointwise/zipped.
constraints: Parallel branches zip to the shortest relevant structure in the intended semantics of the example.
All produced elements must have one common result type.
applicable_when: building matrices or tables from indices
zipping multiple streams in a recurrence
rewriting algorithmic loops into declarative Cryptol

title: Encode running results as `[seed] # [...]`
intent: Represent prefix-dependent computation as a sequence whose first element is the seed and later elements are derived from previous results.
pattern_summary: Create a sequence of accumulated results by prefixing a seed and then generating later elements in lockstep with the input and the accumulated sequence itself.
pattern_template:
ys = [seed] # [ step x y
              | x <- xs
              | y <- ys
              ]
rationale: This is the canonical Cryptol recurrence/fold shape for many stream and scan style computations.
positive_example: ys = [0] # [ max x y | x <- xs | y <- ys ]
variation_notes: The final answer is often `ys ! 0` or `last ys`, while debugging often benefits from returning the whole `ys`.
`step` may consume tuples/records rather than a single element.
anti_pattern: Explicit recursive function definitions for simple prefix accumulations when a recurrence would be clearer and closer to hardware/dataflow style.
constraints: `ys` has one more element than `xs`.
The seed must have the same element type as the recurrence result.

title: Use `foldl` when only the final accumulator matters
intent: Collapse a finite sequence to one value when intermediate running states are not needed as first-class output.
pattern_summary: Use `foldl` with an explicit seed and a state transformer to express an iterative update over a sequence.
pattern_template: result = foldl step init xs
rationale: Compared with a self-referential running sequence, `foldl` is compact and directly states that only the final accumulator is semantically relevant.
positive_example: evalPoly p x = foldl (+) zero terms
variation_notes: If you also need all intermediate states, prefer `scanl` or the explicit `[seed] # [...]` recurrence.
Strict `foldl'` is available when evaluation discipline matters and the accumulator type is in `Eq`.
anti_pattern: Generating a full running-result sequence and then immediately discarding all but the last element when no intermediate states are useful.
constraints: Requires `fin n` on the traversed sequence for the primitive signature.
The accumulator and element roles may be heterogeneous.
applicable_when: reducing rounds/blocks to one final state

title: Use `scanl` when you want every intermediate accumulator
intent: Preserve all intermediate accumulator states in addition to the final value.
pattern_summary: `scanl` is the library version of a seeded running-result recurrence: it returns the initial seed plus every accumulator state after processing each input element.
pattern_template: states = scanl step init xs
rationale: This is a compact alternative to the manual `[seed] # [...]` shape when the update is a standard left scan.
positive_example: scanl : {n, b, a} (b -> a -> b) -> b -> [n]a -> [n+1]b
variation_notes: Good for debugging or when later logic needs every prefix state.
Conceptually equivalent to a recurrence that returns `ys` rather than `ys ! 0`.
anti_pattern: Using `foldl` and then re-running computations just to recover intermediate states.
constraints: Result length is input length plus one for the seed.
applicable_when: constructing prefix states for later reuse

title: Define infinite or open-ended streams with self-reference
intent: Express stream generators and feedback equations declaratively using Cryptol's lazy/self-referential sequence definitions.
pattern_summary: Define a stream in terms of an initial seed concatenated with a new sequence computed from the stream itself and shifted/dropped views of it.
pattern_template:
as = seed # new
  where
    new = [ step a b c
          | a <- as
          | b <- drop`{k1} as
          | c <- drop`{k2} as
          ]
rationale: This is the idiomatic Cryptol encoding of stream equations and feedback systems.
positive_example:
as = [0x3F, 0xE2, 0x65, 0xCA] # new
  where
    new = [ a ^ b ^ c | a <- as | b <- drop`{1} as | c <- drop`{3} as ]
variation_notes: The feedback taps may come from multiple delayed versions of the same stream.
Can be finite-prefix seeded but conceptually infinite.
anti_pattern: Encoding simple feedback streams as low-level mutable state loops external to Cryptol's sequence model.
constraints: Offsets induced by `drop` must be well-typed relative to the seeded stream.
This pattern relies on Cryptol's support for infinite/self-referential sequences.

title: Use parallel comprehensions to thread state and input in lockstep
intent: Process an evolving state sequence and an input sequence together without explicit indices.
pattern_summary: Put one generator over the current input structure and another over the running state structure in parallel branches so each step consumes one state and one input item.
pattern_template: outs = [ step x s | x <- xs | s <- states ]
rationale: This pattern is clearer than manual index arithmetic when a recurrence needs one fresh input and one previous state at each step.
positive_example:
ncrs = [(True, inputChar, initRotor)] #
       [ scramble (notch, char, r)
       | r <- rotors
       | (notch, char, rotor') <- ncrs ]
variation_notes: The state side may be a tuple/record carrying multiple values.
Often paired with a seed prefix and then projected to separate result streams.
anti_pattern: Manually indexing both the input and the state sequence with `@ i` in multiple places for a simple zipped recurrence.
constraints: The recurrence state sequence usually has one more element than the traversed input sequence because of the seed.

title: Treat properties as ordinary Bit-valued functions
intent: Write properties using normal Cryptol expressions, relying on the fact that a property is just a Bit-valued function/declaration.
pattern_summary: A `property` declaration introduces a Bit-valued definition that can be called, typed, checked, or proven like an ordinary function of its arguments.
pattern_template: property <name> args = <bit_expression>
rationale: This keeps the property language identical to the programming language and makes property reuse straightforward.
positive_example: property sqDiffsCorrect (x, y) = sqDiff1 (x, y) == sqDiff2 (x, y)
variation_notes: A property may be parameterless and act like a constant test-vector check.
You can often omit the separate type signature and let Cryptol infer it.
anti_pattern: Introducing an external pseudo-assertion language when ordinary Bit-valued Cryptol expressions suffice.
constraints: The property body must ultimately have type `Bit`.
applicable_when: attaching lightweight contracts to helper functions

title: Express function equality with `===` when comparing extensional behavior
intent: State that two functions produce the same outputs for all inputs of a given type.
pattern_summary: Use the pointwise equality operator `===` to define a property that two functions are extensionally equal, instead of manually re-stating all arguments.
pattern_template: property <name> = f === g
rationale: This is shorter and clearer than explicitly writing `\x -> f x == g x` or unpacking tuples by hand when the goal is whole-function equivalence.
positive_example: property sqDiffsCorrect = sqDiff1 === sqDiff2
variation_notes: When you need side conditions, combine this with a conditional property instead.
`!==` is the extensional disequality counterpart.
anti_pattern: Repeating a long argument list in the property body when the intended statement is simply function equivalence.
constraints: The codomain must support `Eq`.
The monomorphic instance still matters for proving/checking.

title: Encode side conditions with implication or `if ... then ... else True`
intent: Restrict a property to the inputs where its intended precondition holds.
pattern_summary: Write a property in implication form, or explicitly return `True` outside the preconditioned region, so only valid cases are required to satisfy the core assertion.
pattern_template:
property <name> args = precondition ==> core_property

-- or
property <name> args = if precondition then core_property else True
rationale: Many arithmetic and parsing laws are only valid under specific width/domain assumptions.
positive_example: a ==> b = if a then b else True
variation_notes: `\/` and `/\` are also available as logical connectives defined via `if`.
Good for guarding division/modulus by nonzero denominators or message validity predicates.
anti_pattern: Forgetting the side condition and then treating a counterexample outside the intended domain as a bug in the implementation.
constraints: The final property must still return `Bit`.
The side condition should be easy to state from the same inputs.
retrieval_hints: if precondition then prop else True

title: Capture test vectors as a property over a table of known pairs
intent: Turn a list of known input/output examples into an executable property.
pattern_summary: Store test vectors in a sequence and define a property that all table entries satisfy the expected relation, usually by mapping and then checking against `~zero` or by using `all`.
pattern_template:
property <name> = [ f x == y | (x, y) <- testVector ] == ~zero
  where
    testVector = [...]
rationale: This preserves golden examples as executable specifications that evolve with the code base.
positive_example:
property inctest = [ f x == y | (x, y) <- testVector ] == ~zero
  where
    f x = x + 1
    testVector = [(3, 4), (4, 5), (12, 13), (78, 79)]
variation_notes: You may also encode the same idea with `all` over the vector list.
Parameterless properties are convenient for regression checks.
anti_pattern: Keeping example vectors only in comments or external notes where they are not automatically checked.
constraints: The vector format must align with the tested function arity and result type.

title: Specialize polymorphic properties before proving them
intent: Convert a polymorphic property into a concrete instance so the prover can discharge it.
pattern_summary: If a property has a polymorphic type, use an explicit type annotation or type application at the proof site to choose the concrete instance to prove.
rationale: Cryptol does not directly prove a polymorphic proposition as one universal theorem across all instances; you must pick a monomorphic instance.
positive_example: :prove multShift : [16] -> Bit
variation_notes: You can also prove an anonymous lambda once it has a monomorphic type.
This pattern is essential for rapid experimentation with width-dependent laws.
anti_pattern: :prove multShift   // when `multShift` still has a polymorphic type
constraints: The selected instance must satisfy all type constraints.
Counterexamples are then produced at that monomorphic type.

title: Use size-polymorphic signatures to encode shape invariants in types
intent: Move length and shape assumptions into the type signature instead of leaving them implicit in the body.
pattern_summary: Parameterize functions by numeric type variables and element types, then express sequence shapes directly in the input/output types and constraints.
rationale: Cryptol's type system is especially valuable when it states exact widths and relationships such as `[front + back]a -> [front]a`.
positive_example: tail : {n, a} [1 + n]a -> [n]a
variation_notes: Add arithmetic relations like `n >= 1` or `parts * each == total` when needed.
This pattern should be the default for reusable libraries rather than hard-coding widths too early.
anti_pattern: Encoding length assumptions only in comments while using overly concrete or unconstrained types.
constraints: Use `fin n` when the size must be finite.
Some operations also require extra constraints such as `Literal n b` or `Ring a`.
applicable_when: writing reusable helpers over words or sequences
capturing algorithmic size equations in the type system

title: Use backtick notation to turn numeric type parameters into values
intent: Reference a numeric type parameter as a term-level number inside expressions.
pattern_summary: When a computation needs the numeric value of a type-level parameter, use backtick notation such as `` `n ``. This is syntactic sugar for `number`-style demotion.
pattern_template:
f : {n, a, b} (fin n, Literal n b) => [n]a -> b
f _ = `n
rationale: This bridges type-level sizes and term-level logic without introducing ad hoc extra value parameters.
positive_example:
length : {n, a, b} (fin n, Literal n b) => [n]a -> b
length _ = `n
variation_notes: Often used in guards, width calculations, and module parameters.
If context does not fix the result type, Cryptol often defaults to `Integer`.
anti_pattern: Adding a redundant runtime parameter solely to carry a size that is already in the type.
constraints: The demoted type expression must be finite and numeric.
The result type must satisfy the relevant `Literal` constraint.
applicable_when: comparing a size parameter with a constant

title: Use inline argument type declarations to guide inference while prototyping
intent: Constrain argument widths locally even when a full top-level signature is not yet written.
pattern_summary: Annotate function parameters directly in the left-hand side to pin down important parts of the type during early development.
pattern_template:
f (x:[w]a) = <body>
-- or
addBytes (x:[8]) (y:[8]) = x + y
rationale: This is a pragmatic middle ground between no annotations and a full explicit signature, especially in the REPL or early drafting.
positive_example: addBytes (x:[8]) (y:[8]) = x + y
variation_notes: Parentheses around inline typed parameters are mandatory.
Later, stable code should usually receive a full top-level signature as well.
anti_pattern: Leaving a key width unconstrained during prototyping and then chasing confusing inference/defaulting behavior.
constraints: This only annotates the parameter occurrence; additional constraints may still be inferred from the body.

title: Package recurring arithmetic constraints into a named `type constraint` synonym
intent: Avoid repeating the same bundle of size predicates across many signatures.
pattern_summary: Define a named `type constraint` alias and use it in later type signatures exactly where the expanded arithmetic predicate set would otherwise appear.
pattern_template:
type constraint <Name> a b = (<constraints>)

f : {a, b, ...} (<Name> a b, ...) => ...
rationale: Constraint bundles improve readability and make later refactoring of numeric side conditions much easier.
positive_example: type constraint myConstraint a b = (fin a, fin b, b >= width a)
variation_notes: Good for project-wide notions such as valid block sizes or width relations.
Works best when the grouped constraints are semantically meaningful, not merely incidental.
anti_pattern: Copy-pasting the same long tuple of arithmetic constraints across many functions.
constraints: A type constraint synonym expands transparently where used.

title: Use `newtype` when you need a semantically distinct wrapper with controlled operations
intent: Represent domain-specific values as a distinct type rather than a transparent alias when the semantics should not collapse to the underlying representation.
pattern_summary: Declare a `newtype` with a record body, build values via the constructor of the same name, and project fields directly when implementing custom operations.
rationale: Unlike `type`, `newtype` prevents the value from automatically inheriting all the behaviors of the underlying representation and gives a lightweight abstraction barrier.
positive_example: newtype CplxQ = { real : Rational, imag : Rational }
variation_notes: Useful for invariants, semantic distinctions, and custom arithmetic/ordering.
Can be parameterized, e.g. `newtype Poly n a = { coeffs : [n+1]a }`.
anti_pattern: Using a plain `type` synonym for a concept that should be semantically distinct and not freely interchangeable with the raw representation.
constraints: The body of a newtype is a record.
Reasoning properties over arbitrary generated newtype values may require care if invariants are intended but not enforced by the shape alone.
applicable_when: preventing accidental misuse of generic operations
documenting semantic types in crypto specs

title: Use qualified imports to wrap or refine imported definitions without name collisions
intent: Bring a module into scope under a qualifier so local code can define refined or wrapped versions of similar names safely.
pattern_summary: Import a dependency under an alias and reference its members through `Alias::name`, leaving the unqualified local names available for wrappers/refinements.
pattern_template:
import Utils as U

localName args = U::localName args && <extra_condition>
rationale: This pattern prevents accidental clashes and makes provenance explicit in larger multi-file specs.
positive_example:
import utilities as util
all xs = util::all xs && (length xs) > 0
variation_notes: Especially useful when a local helper intentionally refines a common name such as `all`, `map`, or `encrypt`.
Pairs well with hierarchical module names.
anti_pattern: Unqualified import of a module that exports a name you intend to redefine locally.
constraints: Call imported members as `Qualifier::name`.
The import alias becomes part of the retrieval/provenance context.
applicable_when: building refined APIs on top of a base module
avoiding namespace collisions in generated multi-file code

title: Separate public API from internal helpers with a `private` block
intent: Keep helper definitions available inside the module while preventing them from becoming part of the public surface.
pattern_summary: Expose the main declarations first, then place implementation helpers under `private` so client modules only see the intended API.
pattern_template:
module M where

publicDef = ... helper ...

private
  helper = ...
rationale: This keeps generated modules cleaner and reduces accidental coupling across files.
positive_example:
private internalDouble x = x + x
exportedDouble = x * 2
variation_notes: A module may contain multiple private blocks.
Layout matters because `private` introduces a new scope.
anti_pattern: Exporting every helper by default and relying on naming conventions alone to signal internal-only status.
constraints: Private declarations remain usable inside the defining module.
The keyword introduces a new layout scope.
applicable_when: large generated modules with internal plumbing
separating spec-facing API from derived utilities

title: Mirror directory structure with hierarchical module names
intent: Organize a multi-file Cryptol project into namespaces that reflect the filesystem layout.
pattern_summary: Name modules with `::`-separated components so the loader can locate files by directory structure and the project gains explicit namespacing.
pattern_template:
module A::B::C where

import A::X
rationale: This scales much better than a flat namespace once the project contains multiple algorithm families and shared utilities.
positive_example: module Hash::SHA3 where
variation_notes: Good for separating algorithm families, utility libraries, and proof/property modules.
Qualified imports compose naturally with hierarchical names.
anti_pattern: Keeping all modules flat even when they belong to different algorithm families or layers.
constraints: The loader resolves the hierarchy via the directory tree under `CRYPTOLPATH` or the current search path.

title: Use `parameter` blocks for width- or data-parameterized modules
intent: Define a reusable module whose constants, widths, or helper values are supplied externally.
pattern_summary: Introduce a `parameter` block inside a module to declare type and value parameters, then write the module body against those abstract names.
pattern_template:
module M where
  parameter
    type n : #
    x : [n]

  f : [n]
  f = x + 1
rationale: This gives a light-weight functor style directly in Cryptol without needing a full external interface file for simple cases.
positive_example:
module M where
  parameter
    type n : #
    type constraint (fin n, n >= 1)
    x : [n]

  f : [n]
  f = 1 + x
variation_notes: Later instantiate with a concrete module or anonymous `where` block.
Useful for parameter sets, constants, and round-dependent tables.
anti_pattern: Copying nearly identical modules for each width/parameter set instead of abstracting the differing pieces.
constraints: Parameters may include type declarations, value declarations, and type constraints.
The parameter block behaves like an anonymous interface.
applicable_when: generic building blocks reused at several widths
separating algorithm logic from concrete constants

title: Encode substitution/permutation ciphers as indexed lookup tables
intent: Represent a finite alphabet mapping as a sequence/string and implement substitution by index arithmetic plus lookup.
pattern_summary: Build a permutation table in sequence order and translate a symbol into an index, then look up the substituted output with `@`.
pattern_template: substFwd (perm, c) = perm @ (c - 'A')
rationale: This is the natural Cryptol form of many alphabetic and byte-permutation transforms.
positive_example: shift c = map @ (c - 'A')
variation_notes: The map itself may be static, rotated, or computed from a key.
This pattern generalizes from characters to bytes and small words.
anti_pattern: Encoding a fixed finite substitution as a long chain of nested `if`/`case` conditions.
constraints: The source symbol must be represented in a way compatible with the chosen base index (`'A'` for alphabet examples).
The permutation table length must match the input alphabet/domain.
applicable_when: S-box-like table access over a small domain

title: Recover inverse substitution by searching the table, often via a fold-style helper
intent: Compute the preimage of a substituted symbol when the forward map is easier to store than the inverse map.
pattern_summary: Define an inverse helper that scans a permutation/lookup structure to find which source symbol maps to the target symbol, then reuse it in decryption.
pattern_template:
invSubst : (Permutation, Char) -> Char
invSubst (perm, c) = <search perm for source that maps to c>
rationale: Many toy and educational crypto specs present the forward substitution first, but decryption needs the inverse mapping.
positive_example: substBwd (perm, c) = invSubst (perm, c)
variation_notes: For small domains, linear search via fold is often acceptable and clear.
For heavier use, you may precompute an inverse table and then use the forward lookup pattern again.
anti_pattern: Duplicating a second hard-coded inverse table by hand when it can be derived or computed from the forward permutation.
constraints: The forward permutation should be injective on the intended domain for a clean inverse.
If search may fail, decide on an explicit fallback value or use `Option`/`Result`.

title: Write encryption rounds as a linear named pipeline in a `where` block
intent: Make multi-stage transformations readable by naming each stage in order.
pattern_summary: Introduce intermediate names in a `where` block so each step of the transform corresponds to one line, preserving the conceptual algorithm pipeline.
pattern_template:
algo input = out
  where
    s1 = step1 input
    s2 = step2 s1
    s3 = step3 s2
    out = step4 s3
rationale: This closely matches algorithm descriptions and is much easier to debug and verify than deeply nested compositions.
positive_example:
enigmaLoop (pboard, rotors, refl, c0) = (rotors', c5)
  where
    c1 = substFwd (pboard, c0)
    (rotors', c2) = joinRotors (rotors, c1)
    c3 = substFwd (refl, c2)
    c4 = backSignal(rotors, c3)
    c5 = substBwd (pboard, c4)
variation_notes: Pattern works equally well for block-cipher rounds, parser pipelines, and encode/decode passes.
Intermediate names should reflect semantic stages, not arbitrary temporary variables.
anti_pattern: Compressing a multi-stage algorithm into one unreadable nested expression when the stages are conceptually distinct.
constraints: Each stage should have a stable semantic role and ideally a checkable type.
applicable_when: bridging pseudocode steps to Cryptol code
retrieval_hints: c1 c2 c3 where block pipeline

title: State correctness of cipher pairs as decrypt-after-encrypt identity
intent: Capture the core correctness contract of a reversible cipher in one executable property.
pattern_summary: Write a property saying that decrypting an encrypted valid message under the same key/state yields the original message, usually with a validity side condition on the message domain.
pattern_template: property cipherCorrect key msg = validMessage msg ==> dCipher (key, cipher (key, msg)) == msg
rationale: This is the most direct and reusable correctness statement for reversible transforms.
positive_example:
dEnigma = enigma
-- self-decrypting machine pattern

-- generalized property shape:
property cipherCorrect key msg = validMessage msg ==> dCipher (key, cipher (key, msg)) == msg
variation_notes: If encryption and decryption are literally the same function, express that explicitly and then prove the round-trip property.
For stateful machines, be clear whether the machine is reinitialized between calls.
anti_pattern: Checking only a few examples of encryption/decryption agreement and treating that as the full correctness contract.
constraints: May require message-domain predicates such as uppercase-only alphabetic strings.
Polymorphic message lengths usually need a monomorphic instantiation at proof time.

title: Represent machine/state evolution as a sequence of `(state, output)` pairs
intent: Model a stateful encryptor as a recurrence that threads state and emits one output per input symbol.
pattern_summary: Seed a sequence with the initial state and a placeholder output, then compute one `(next_state, output)` pair per input symbol, finally projecting the outputs.
pattern_template:
pairs = [(initState, seedOut)] #
        [ step state input
        | input <- inputs
        | (state, _) <- pairs
        ]
outputs = tail [ out | (_, out) <- pairs ]
rationale: This is an idiomatic way to model finite-state machines without mutable variables.
positive_example:
rcs = [(m.rotors, '*')] #
      [ enigmaLoop (m.plugboard, r, m.reflector, c)
      | c <- pt
      | (r, _) <- rcs
      ]

enigma (m, pt) = tail [ c | (_, c) <- rcs ]
variation_notes: The threaded state may be a record rather than a tuple.
Often combines with a separate round pipeline function for one symbol/block.
anti_pattern: Hand-managing state updates outside a recurrence when the process is one-step-per-input and purely functional.
constraints: The seed output is usually a placeholder and should be removed with `tail` or equivalent projection.
The step function must consume exactly one prior state and one new input.

title: Use type-level arithmetic in finite sequence enumeration bounds
intent: Generate finite sequences whose length depends on a type parameter, using only type-level expressions in the bounds.
pattern_summary: In [a .. b], both a and b must be type expressions, NOT value expressions. You cannot write [0 .. `n - 1] because `n - 1 is a value-level expression. Instead use [0 .. (n-1)] where n is a type variable (no backtick), or use take with a type application.
rationale: The Cryptol parser rejects value-level arithmetic in finite enumeration bounds. Use take or result-type-driven truncation.
positive_example:
indices : {n} (fin n, n >= 1) => [n]Integer
indices = take`{n} [0 ...]

ranged : {n} (fin n, n >= 1) => [n][8]
ranged = [0 .. (n-1)]
constraints: In [a..b], a and b are type expressions.
Backtick demotes to value, rejected in bounds.
Use take`{n} [0...] or result type [n]a.
applicable_when: generating index sequences from type variables
fixing boundaries of .. sequences should be valid numeric types error

title: Backtick demotion: when and where to use `n to convert types to values
intent: Correctly use backtick notation to convert type parameters to runtime values.
pattern_summary: Use `n in expressions (arithmetic, indexing). Do NOT use `n in sequence enumeration bounds [a..b].
rationale: Backtick converts a type to a value. Enumeration bounds require types, not values.
positive_example:
len : {n} (fin n, Literal n Integer) => [n][8] -> Integer
len _ = `n
anti_pattern: [0 .. `n - 1]  // WRONG in enumeration bounds
constraints: `n valid in: function bodies, arithmetic.
NOT valid in: enumeration bounds [a..b].
applicable_when: using type parameter in runtime arithmetic
fixing Expected a value named X but found a type instead

title: Convert imperative mutable array updates to functional style using updates or foldl
intent: Transform imperative algorithms with mutable array writes into pure functional Cryptol.
pattern_summary: Cryptol arrays are immutable. Use updates (batch), foldl (sequential state threading), or comprehension rebuilds.
rationale: Imperative pseudocode uses mutable arrays but Cryptol is purely functional.
positive_example:
butterfly : [8][16] -> [8][16]
butterfly arr = updates arr [0..3] [ (arr@i)+(arr@(i+4)) | i <- [0..3] ]
anti_pattern: arr[j] = value  // WRONG: no assignment in Cryptol
constraints: updates : {n,a,ix} (fin n, Integral ix) => [n]a -> [m]ix -> [m]a -> [n]a
Use foldl for sequential deps.

title: Convert nested imperative loops to nested foldl calls
intent: Transform multi-level nested imperative loops into nested foldl compositions.
pattern_summary: Each loop level becomes a foldl that threads the array state.
pattern_template:
result = foldl outerStep initArray lens
  where
    outerStep arr len = foldl middleStep arr starts
      where starts = ...
    middleStep arr start = foldl innerStep arr js
      where js = ...
    innerStep arr j = updates arr [j, j+len] [newJ, newJLen]
rationale: NTT algorithms have triple-nested loops with interdependent state.
positive_example:
ntt arr = foldl layerStep arr [0..6]
  where layerStep a layer = foldl groupStep a groups
anti_pattern: // Imperative assignment inside loops
constraints: Each foldl level threads the array.
Use updates for modifications.

title: Ensure consistent bitvector widths with zero-extension
intent: Fix type mismatch errors from mixing bitvector widths.
pattern_summary: Use zext or zero # x to widen narrower bitvectors.
pattern_template:
wider : [9]
wider = zext narrowVal  // narrowVal : [7]

wider2 : [9]
wider2 = (zero : [2]) # narrowVal
rationale: Cryptol requires exact width matching.
positive_example:
len : [9]
len = zext (2 ^^ (layer + 1) : [8])
anti_pattern: start + len  // start:[9], len:[7] => type mismatch
constraints: zext : {m,n} (fin m, fin n, m >= n) => [n] -> [m]
applicable_when: fixing Type mismatch Expected N Inferred M errors

title: ML-KEM NTT zeta precomputed table mod 3329
intent: Provide precomputed zeta values for ML-KEM NTT.
pattern_summary: zeta=17 is a primitive 256th root of unity mod q=3329. 128 precomputed zeta powers indexed by BitRev7.
rationale: NTT and NTT_inverse reference zeta^BitRev7(i) mod q without providing the table.
positive_example:
zetas : [128][12]
zetas = [1, 1729, 2580, ...]

BitRev7 : [8] -> [8]
BitRev7 i = zext (reverse (drop`{1} (i : [8])))
anti_pattern: zetas = undefined
constraints: 128 entries.
Values fit in [12].

title: Thread heterogeneous loop state with a tuple or record accumulator
intent: Model loop state that mixes arrays, counters, and flags by threading a tuple or record accumulator through recursion or foldl.
pattern_summary: When state fields have different types, use `(arr, idx, flag)` or a record and update that structure on each step. Do not package mixed-type state as a sequence.
pattern_template:
step : (STATE_A, STATE_B) -> STEP_INPUT -> (STATE_A, STATE_B)
step (a, b) x = (a', b')
  where
    a' = ...
    b' = ...

result = foldl step initState inputs
rationale: This avoids type-unification failures and makes state transitions explicit.
positive_example:
processLayer : ([256][12], [8]) -> [8] -> ([256][12], [8])
processLayer (f_hat, zetaIdx) layer = (f_hat', zetaIdx')
variation_notes: A record is often clearer when the state has three or more fields.
Works equally well with explicit recursion instead of `foldl`.
anti_pattern: state = [arr, idx]   // mixed element types forced into one sequence
constraints: Choose tuples for small local state; prefer records when field names improve clarity.
applicable_when: translating imperative loops with array state plus counters

title: Choose one loop translation strategy: static literal schedule or value-level recursion
intent: Avoid mixing type-driven finite sequence construction with runtime loop control in the same translated algorithm.
pattern_summary: For loop-heavy algorithms, decide first whether the bounds are fully static. If yes, use literal schedules and fixed finite sequences. If bounds are computed at runtime or from intermediate values, use explicit value-level recursion instead of dynamically sized finite sequences.
pattern_template:
// Strategy A: static schedule
lens = [128, 64, 32, 16, 8, 4, 2]
out = foldl step init lens

// Strategy B: value-level recursion
loop idx state = if done idx then state else loop idx' state'
  where
    state' = ...
rationale: Mixing the two styles is a common source of ambiguous numeric types and invalid type/value interactions.
positive_example:
loop start state = if start >= limit then state else loop (start + stride) state'
  where state' = ...
variation_notes: Use static schedules when the specification literally fixes the layer list.
Use value-level recursion when counts depend on current values such as `len` or `numBlocks`.
anti_pattern: Using `take len [0 ...]` or `[0 .. (len - 1)]` when `len` is a runtime value computed inside the algorithm.
constraints: Pick one strategy per function body unless the boundary between them is extremely explicit.

title: Use value-level recursion when an iteration count is computed from values
intent: Translate loops whose counts come from values by explicit recursion over a runtime counter or index.
pattern_summary: When a loop bound is a value such as `len`, `numBlocks`, or `remaining`, write a recursive helper `go i state` rather than trying to materialize a finite sequence of that length.
pattern_template:
go i state =
  if i == stop then state
  else go (i + 1) state'
    where
      state' = ... i ... state
rationale: This avoids forcing runtime counts into type-level sequence lengths.
positive_example:
butterflyLoop j state =
  if j == end then state
  else butterflyLoop (j + 1) state'
    where
      state' = step j state
variation_notes: A decreasing counter works as well as an increasing index.
Often pairs naturally with a tuple accumulator.
anti_pattern: js = [0 .. (len - 1)]   // `len` is a value, not a static type-level length
constraints: Ensure the recursion variable and termination test live in the same numeric domain.
applicable_when: repairing `Ambiguous numeric type` from dynamic counts

title: Keep generator-dependent calculations inside the comprehension or pass the generator variable explicitly
intent: Avoid scope errors when a comprehension element needs a derived value based on the generator variable.
pattern_summary: Either compute the derived quantity inline inside the comprehension element, or define a helper function that explicitly takes the generator-bound variable as an argument.
pattern_template:
xs = [ elem i | i <- idxs ]
  where
    elem i = ... i ...
rationale: This preserves lexical scope and avoids `Value not in scope` in trailing `where` blocks.
positive_example:
coeffs = [ coeff i | i <- [0 .. 255] ]
  where
    coeff i = bits @ (base i)
variation_notes: Inlining is fine for short expressions; helper functions are better for repeated structure.
anti_pattern:
xs = [ y | i <- idxs ]
  where
    y = f i
constraints: The helper must take every short-lived variable it depends on as an explicit parameter.

title: Separate index-domain helpers from arithmetic-domain helpers
intent: Keep array indexing and arithmetic/modular computation in clearly separated domains, with explicit conversion points.
pattern_summary: Use one small helper family for index calculations and another for arithmetic. Convert at the boundary instead of mixing index arithmetic, `Bit`, words, and `Integer` in one expression.
pattern_template:
idxStep : IDX -> IDX
idxStep i = ...

termValue : WORD -> Integer
termValue w = ...

result = arr @ idx
  where
    idx = idxStep base
rationale: This makes width requirements explicit and greatly reduces cascading type mismatches.
positive_example:
idx2 = idx1 + len
acc = (termA + termB) % modulus
variation_notes: A tiny `bitToInteger` helper is often worthwhile when summing bits.
For [256] arrays, design all index-producing expressions together.
anti_pattern: sum [ zext (bits @ (base + j)) * 2^j | ... ]
constraints: Widen narrow index fragments before combining them with larger offsets.

title: Use tiny local helpers to normalize Bit, word, and Integer conversions
intent: Prefer explicit local conversion helpers over implicit mixed-domain arithmetic in one line.
pattern_summary: When an algorithm needs to interpret bits as coefficients or counters, define a short helper with an explicit type and reuse it.
pattern_template:
bitToInt : Bit -> Integer
bitToInt b = if b then 1 else 0

coeff i = sum [ bitToInt (bits @ j) * weight j | j <- ... ]
rationale: This is easier for both the type checker and retrieval-guided repair than deeply nested coercions.
positive_example:
bitToInt : Bit -> Integer
bitToInt b = if b then 1 else 0
variation_notes: Use a word-widening helper separately from Bit-to-Integer conversion.
A named helper also makes unresolved semantics easier to audit.
anti_pattern: sum [ zext (bitExpr) * complicatedWeight | ... ]
constraints: Give each helper an explicit type signature.

title: Use a conservative unresolved skeleton when a required external primitive is unavailable
intent: Keep generated code legal and honest when a core primitive or dependency is missing from the available evidence.
pattern_summary: If a required external function, type, or module is not actually provided, emit a minimal skeleton that marks the dependency as unresolved instead of writing a pseudo-complete body that references undefined names.
pattern_template:
module M where

// UNRESOLVED: external primitive `PRIM` was not provided in the available context.
foo : INPUT -> OUTPUT
foo x = undefined
rationale: A conservative skeleton is easier to repair and more trustworthy than a body built around commented assumptions.
variation_notes: If the dependency is only one helper, a typed local hole may be enough.
Prefer real imports or provided dependency code when available.
anti_pattern:
// assume PRIM exists
foo x = PRIM x
constraints: Only use this when the missing dependency is genuinely unavailable in the current evidence.
applicable_when: repairing `not in scope` on external dependencies

title: Prefer precomputed table lookup when the specification already provides the constant table
intent: Avoid re-deriving values at generation time when the algorithm already comes with an authoritative constant table.
pattern_summary: If the spec or parameter context includes a constant table such as permutation data, S-boxes, or NTT zetas, retrieve and index that table directly instead of inventing a helper to recompute the same values.
pattern_template:
table : [N]WORD
table = [...]

value = table @ idx
rationale: Direct lookup is usually simpler, more accurate, and easier to verify than rebuilding the constant from a secondary formula.
positive_example: zeta = zetas @ idx
variation_notes: Keep the table in a separate constant-block example when it is long.
Pair with a bit-reversal or zero-based index helper when the spec uses transformed indices.
anti_pattern: zeta = zeta_pow idx   // when an authoritative `zetas` table is already available
constraints: Only use direct lookup when the provided table is authoritative for the algorithm variant.
